title: Google starts lowering Play Store fees, making good on Epic Games settlement
gist: Google is implementing Play Store fee reductions mandated by its global settlement with Epic Games, beginning June 30 in Europe, the UK, and the US. The new structure splits commissions into service (10% flat for the first $1M annually, up to 25% above that) and billing (5%) components and allows developers to redirect users to external payment options. A second, more disruptive settlement phase—requiring Google to certify and support third-party app stores on Android devices—has not yet been addressed.

insights.claim: Starting June 30, Google is restructuring Play Store commissions into two components: a flat 10% service fee on the first $1 million in developer earnings annually, and a 5% billing fee on all transactions, with maximum rates of 25% for existing installs and 20% for new installs after June 30.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Small developers earning under $1M annually now face a maximum 15% effective rate (10%+5%) versus the prior 30%, improving their unit economics on the Play Store.
insights.quote: The biggest win for small developers is the new flat 10 percent service fee for the first $1 million in earnings every year

insights.claim: For the first time, Google will allow Play Store developers to redirect users to external payment processors via a choice screen; developers still pay the service fee on these transactions but avoid the 5% billing fee.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: External payment routing gives developers and third-party payment processors a legitimate path to avoid Google's billing infrastructure—directly reducing Google's payment processing revenue.
insights.quote: Developers will finally be allowed to send users outside the Play Store to complete a transaction

insights.claim: The new Play Store fee structure will roll out globally in phases: Europe, UK, and US on June 30; Australia on September 30; Japan and Korea on December 31; and all other regions by September 30, 2027.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The phased global rollout gives Google time to manage revenue impact across regions; developers in later-phase markets continue on old terms through 2027.
insights.quote: Australia will join the new fee structure on September 30, and on December 31, Japan and Korea will be on board. All other regions will move to the new system on September 30, 2027.

insights.claim: The settlement's second phase—requiring Google to certify third-party app stores and allow them to operate more like the Play Store on Android devices—has not been addressed yet and may take longer to implement.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The more structurally disruptive settlement requirement (third-party stores) remains pending; it represents the bigger long-term risk to Google's Android ecosystem control.
insights.quote: That may take longer to implement, particularly as Google also begins enforcing developer verification over the coming months.

insights.claim: Epic added external billing to Fortnite on Android and iOS in 2020 in violation of platform rules, triggering removal from both app stores and lawsuits; Apple largely won its case while Google lost due to how it managed the Play Store with a 'more open appearance.'
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The asymmetric legal outcomes for Apple vs. Google on similar billing disputes highlight that Google's hybrid openness strategy was a legal liability, not an advantage.
insights.quote: Apple managed to (mostly) win its case, but Google tripped up in how it tried to control the Play Store while keeping a more open appearance.
